content:
First note that if \(n\) has \(d\) digits then 
\[
\text{(product of digits)}\;\;f(n)\;\le 9^d,
\]
whereas
\[
n^2-15n-27\;\sim\;n^2\quad\text{for large }n.
\]
A quick “size‐check’’ shows that no solutions can occur once \(n\) exceeds 20 (for two digits) or 36 (for three digits), and in fact testing \(1\le n\le20\) by hand one finds the first nonnegative RHS at \(n=17\), namely
\[
1\cdot7=7
\quad\text{and}\quad
17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.
\]
All other \(n<20\) give a mismatch (or a negative RHS), and for \(n>20\) the RHS grows too fast to catch up with the product of digits.  Thus the only positive solution is \(n=17\), and the requested sum is

\(\boxed{17}\).
